title: Pasta consumes high load CPU
body:
### Issue Description

Podman rootless container with Qbitorrent image, and Pasta network, the Pasta process consumes high load of CPU (higher than 90%).

It only occurs when downloading in high speed.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue:
1. Create a rootless qbitorrent  container
```
podman run --rm --name qbit-test -p 8080:8080 -v ./Descargas:/downloads lscr.io/linuxserver/qbittorrent:latest
```
2. Start downloading 1-2 torrents with lots of seeders 
3. Open htop/top and see how passt.avx2 process consumes CPU.


### Describe the results you received

```
ps aux | grep -i pasta
/usr/bin/pasta --config-net -t 8080-8080:8080-8080 --dns-forward 169.254.1.1 -u none -T none -U none --no-map-gw --quiet --netns /run/user/1000/netns/netns-474aff30-f3e2-6ae9-08b7-b7e1ab6fc549 --map-guest-addr 169.254.1.2
```

In top:
```
    PID   USUARIO   PR  NI    VIRT          RES    SHR S  %CPU  %MEM     HORA+ ORDEN                                                                                                                       
 171957       yuki  20   0  194008  36036   1384 R     96,8       0,2       2:43.08 passt.avx2 
```

### Describe the results you expected

I assume that the  pasta process should be not so  high.

I can replicate using a container with iperf3. Pasta goes up to 90% and then down a bit.

```
podman run -p 5201:5201 -it openeuler/iperf /bin/bash   

Welcome to 7.0.12-zen1-1-zen

Users online: 	0


[root@df0cf3308e2f /]# iperf3 -s
```

If in the same container, using more ports, starts more iperf3, and in the host starts more clients, the pasta process consumes more CPU.

```
yuki      176601 17.7  0.1 194008 25176 ?        Rs   10:37   0:12 /usr/bin/pasta --config-net -t 5201-5203:5201-5203 --dns-forward 169.254.1.1 -u none -T none -U none --no-map-gw --quiet --netns /run/user/1000/netns/netns-5bd61a76-f2cf-3eb8-2e49-801b27b9083a --map-guest-addr 169.254.1.2
```

My CPU is a Intel(R) Core(TM) i5-9400 CPU @ 2.90GHz.

```
uname -a
Linux archy 7.0.12-zen1-1-zen #1 ZEN SMP PREEMPT_DYNAMIC Wed, 10 Jun 2026 08:57:50 +0000 x86_64 GNU/Linux
```

and I can replicate it in "normal" kernel:

```
uname -a
Linux archy 7.0.12-arch1-1 #1 SMP PREEMPT_DYNAMIC Wed, 10 Jun 2026 08:58:02 +0000 x86_64 GNU/Linux
```
Background: this was first reported  in #23739, but split out to a new bug, because we were primarily investigating a different symptom there.

High load with iperf3 is, to a certain extent, expected: iperf3 is sending data as fast as it possibly can.  Running locally, it's not constrained by a physical network, so it will eventually run into some sort of local processing limit.  Inside `pasta` is pretty much where I'd expect that, particularly without a backend better than plain `/dev/net/tun` (we have some ideas for this, but they're not close to implementation).  Note also:

- pasta load doesn't necessarily grow in proportion to throughput.  In a sense it's naturally regulating: the higher the load, the less often we'll be woken, meaning we'll transfer more data during each wakeup cycle, which reduces the load we'll impose per byte or packet.
- This isn't really an apples to apples comparison: `iperf3` is sending a lot of data over a single, host to guest TCP connection.  BitTorrent, by contrast could be sending data in either or both directions, over a large number of connections, each of which probably has fairly constrained throughput.  AIUI those connections could be either TCP or UDP, I'm not sure which is more common in practice.

It's possible in this case that the high load is just due to a lot of total throughput from qtbittorrent, That would be a bit surprising, though, since that should be constrained by the external network.  So we'll keep looking to see if there's a bug in play.  Here's some details to start with:

- Do you have a measure of how busy qtbittorrent is?  How much data (bytes and or packets) is it sending & receiving per second?  How many concurrent connections is it maintaining?
- What's the total capacity of your uplink?
- Is qtbittorrent woorking as expected, apart from the high load?
- What proportion of qtbittorrent's connections are TCP versus UDP?  If qtbittorrent is the only major network application running on the host `ss -t -u -n` should give an idea of this.  (This could be relevant, because for TCP we're able to use a high MTU/MSS to the guest, even if it's constrained externally - this can be an important load mitigation factor).

@yuki, we have another report of high load with a multicast protocol.  I don't know if it's the same as the problem here yet, but it's possible.  One interesting detail is that that problem seems to be specific to the "spliced" interface, and goes away with `--no-splice`.  It would be interesting to try adding the `--no-splice` option in your case and seeing if that changes the symptoms.

If I run the parameter `--no-splice`  I got an error:

```
yuki@archy :~$ podman run --rm --name qbit-test -p 8080:8080 --no-splice -v ./Descargas:/downloads lscr.io/linuxserver/qbittorrent:latest
Error: unknown flag: --no-splice
``` 

I  have tried to search  in documentation, but I don't see nothing

> If I run the parameter `--no-splice` I got an error:
> 
> ```
> yuki@archy :~$ podman run --rm --name qbit-test -p 8080:8080 --no-splice -v ./Descargas:/downloads lscr.io/linuxserver/qbittorrent:latest
> Error: unknown flag: --no-splice
> ```

Sorry, I should have been clearer.  That's a pasta option, not a podman option. To pass it to pasta via podman you need to add:
```--network=pasta:--no-splice```
to the podman command line.

Thanks for the `ss` output.  Looks like the overwhelming majority of the activity is over UDP.  That's useful information

I've been dealing with some other high priority issues, so I'm afraid I haven't had a chance to look at this more, I'll get back to it when I can.

Make sense it is a pasta parameter 🤦 

I don't see any change in CPU consumption, it goes to 88-94%

```
ps aux | grep pasta
yuki      192789  0.1  0.2 2196512 47952 pts/0   Sl+  07:08   0:00 podman run --rm --name qbit-test --network=pasta:--no-splice -p 8080:8080 -v ./Descargas:/downloads lscr.io/linuxserver/qbittorrent:latest
yuki      192815 71.9  0.2 194004 34636 ?        Rs   07:08   2:24 /usr/bin/pasta --config-net --no-splice -t 8080-8080:8080-8080 --dns-forward 169.254.1.1 -u none -T none -U none --no-map-gw --quiet --netns /run/user/1000/netns/netns-3ef4136c-49dc-36e5-d64a-690228d9d700 --map-guest-addr 169.254.1.2
```

But I have made some tests and  the CPU goes up if the download rate is unlimited. I have  made the test downloading the latest Ubuntu Desktop ISO torrent:

- Start the download without limit: pasta process uses ~94% CPU, downloading at 56MiB/s
- Limit  the download to 250KiB/s the process down to 3-7%
- Increasing  the download limit to 1000KiB/s the process uses 11-16% of CPU
- Limiting again to 250KiB/s the CPU goes down too.

> Make sense it is a pasta parameter 🤦
> 
> I don't see any change in CPU consumption, it goes to 88-94%

Ok, this is unlikely to be the same as the other bug I mentioned then.  Pity, but useful to know.

> ```
> ps aux | grep pasta
> yuki      192789  0.1  0.2 2196512 47952 pts/0   Sl+  07:08   0:00 podman run --rm --name qbit-test --network=pasta:--no-splice -p 8080:8080 -v ./Descargas:/downloads lscr.io/linuxserver/qbittorrent:latest
> yuki      192815 71.9  0.2 194004 34636 ?        Rs   07:08   2:24 /usr/bin/pasta --config-net --no-splice -t 8080-8080:8080-8080 --dns-forward 169.254.1.1 -u none -T none -U none --no-map-gw --quiet --netns /run/user/1000/netns/netns-3ef4136c-49dc-36e5-d64a-690228d9d700 --map-guest-addr 169.254.1.2
> ```
> 
> But I have made some tests and the CPU goes up if the download rate is unlimited. I have made the test downloading the latest Ubuntu Desktop ISO torrent:
> 
> * Start the download without limit: pasta process uses ~94% CPU, downloading at 56MiB/s
> * Limit  the download to 250KiB/s the process down to 3-7%
> * Increasing  the download limit to 1000KiB/s the process uses 11-16% of CPU
> * Limiting again to 250KiB/s the CPU goes down too.

Ok, looks like the CPU load is related to overall traffic.  That means it's probably not due to something as simple as pasta looping for no reason.  I'll need to look closer when I get the chance.
